startScreenOptions.title: AN55.1-AN55.2 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 3 embedded questions for AN55.1-AN55.2.

interactions.label: In the clinical scenario, the intern presses at McBurney's point and the patient

params.question: In the clinical scenario, the intern presses at McBurney's point and the patient winces. What anatomical fact makes this surface point diagnostically valuable for appendicitis?

answers.text: The appendix is fixed at this location and cannot vary in position

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The appendix actually varies considerably in position (retrocaecal, pelvic, etc.), but its base remains relatively fixed at the caecum, which projects to McBurney's point.

answers.text: The appendicular artery passes superficially here, making it sensitive to pressure

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The appendicular artery is a small branch of the ileocolic artery running in the mesoappendix. It does not pass superficially at this point.

answers.text: The base of the appendix projects to this surface point, so inflammation causes localised tenderness here

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. McBurney's point marks where the base of the appendix projects onto the anterior abdominal wall. When the appendix is inflamed, pressing here produces localised tenderness because the inflamed organ lies directly beneath.

answers.text: The peritoneum is thinnest at this point, allowing direct palpation of the appendix

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The peritoneum has fairly uniform thickness across the abdominal wall. McBurney's point is significant because of the organ that lies beneath it, not because of peritoneal thickness.

overallFeedback.feedback: McBurney's point lies two-thirds from the umbilicus to the right ASIS and marks the base of the appendix. Localised tenderness here strongly suggests appendicitis.
Well done! McBurney's point is a surface landmark every clinician must know — it bridges anatomical knowledge to bedside diagnosis.

interactions.label: What anatomical relationship at the deep inguinal ring distinguishes an indirect

params.question: What anatomical relationship at the deep inguinal ring distinguishes an indirect inguinal hernia from a direct hernia?

answers.text: Indirect hernias are superior to the inguinal ligament; direct hernias are inferior

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Both indirect and direct inguinal hernias pass above the inguinal ligament through the inguinal canal. Hernias passing below the ligament are femoral hernias.

answers.text: Indirect hernias pass lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels; direct hernias pass medial

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The inferior epigastric vessels are the key landmark. An indirect hernia enters through the deep ring lateral to these vessels, while a direct hernia pushes through the posterior wall of the canal medial to them.

answers.text: Indirect hernias are covered by peritoneum; direct hernias are not

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Both types of inguinal hernia have a peritoneal sac. The distinguishing feature is their relationship to the inferior epigastric vessels, not peritoneal covering.

answers.text: Indirect hernias pass through the transversalis fascia; direct hernias pass through the external oblique

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The deep inguinal ring is an opening in the transversalis fascia. Both types of hernia ultimately traverse the same fascial layers; their point of entry relative to the inferior epigastric vessels is the key difference.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember: the inferior epigastric vessels are the anatomical dividing line. Indirect hernias pass lateral to them through the deep ring, while direct hernias pass medial.
Excellent! The inferior epigastric vessels are the key landmark for classifying inguinal hernias — lateral means indirect, medial means direct.

metadata.title: What anatomical relationship at the deep

interactions.label: The right kidney spans T12 to L3, while the left kidney spans T11 to L3. What ex

params.question: The right kidney spans T12 to L3, while the left kidney spans T11 to L3. What explains the lower position of the right kidney?

answers.text: The left renal vein is shorter, pulling the left kidney upward

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The left renal vein is actually longer than the right as it crosses anterior to the aorta. Vein length is a consequence of kidney position, not the cause.

answers.text: The spleen supports the left kidney at a higher position

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The spleen lies lateral to the left kidney in the left hypochondrium but does not elevate or support it. The left kidney is simply not displaced downward as the right is.

answers.text: The right adrenal gland is heavier and pushes the kidney downward

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Both adrenal glands are small organs sitting atop the kidneys. They do not exert significant mass effect. The liver is the structure responsible for the right kidney's lower position.

answers.text: The liver occupies the right hypochondrium and displaces the right kidney inferiorly

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The large right lobe of the liver sits in the right hypochondrium and pushes the right kidney approximately one vertebral level lower than the left.

overallFeedback.feedback: The liver, being the largest abdominal organ, occupies the right hypochondrium and displaces the right kidney about one vertebral level lower than the left.
Correct! The liver's mass effect on the right kidney is a clinically important asymmetry to remember for imaging and examination.
